nativeRange.habitat: The Blue Tang inhabits clear, current-swept reef terraces along the seaward edges of coral reefs. Juveniles are commonly found sheltering in small groups around isolated heads of Pocillopora coral.

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous as an adult, grazing on marine algae; juveniles are largely planktivorous. In the aquarium it is generally advised to offer dried marine algae or nori on a veggie clip several times per week along with vitamin-enriched frozen herbivore preparations, and occasional meaty fare such as mysis or enriched brine shrimp to round out the diet.

avoidWith: Other Paracanthurus hepatus (unless introduced together in a very large system)
Other surgeonfish with similar body shape

summary: The Blue Tang (Paracanthurus hepatus) is a vividly colored surgeonfish native to Indo-Pacific reefs, widely known in popular culture as Dory. Adults grow to around 25–31 cm and are best kept in large, mature reef systems of at least 700 L by aquarists comfortable with active, disease-prone fish.
careNotes: The Blue Tang is a popular but demanding reef fish that reaches roughly 25–31 cm as an adult. Because it is an active, open-water swimmer, a long aquarium of at least 700 L with plenty of unobstructed swimming space and live rockwork for shelter is generally advised. Water is typically kept around 23–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, and a specific gravity of 1.020–1.026. Like all surgeonfish, the Blue Tang carries a sharp, scalpel-like spine at the base of the tail (the caudal peduncle) that can injure both handlers and tankmates; using a container to catch the fish rather than a bare net is recommended. Blue Tangs are notably susceptible to marine ich (Cryptocaryon irritans) and marine velvet, two of the most common saltwater fish diseases, and are also prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and lateral line, typically linked to poor water quality or a diet lacking marine algae and vitamins. A quarantine period before introduction to the display tank and a varied, algae-rich diet are strongly advised. The species is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward unrelated fish, but tends to be highly aggressive toward other Blue Tangs and surgeonfish of similar body shape, so it is usually kept singly unless multiple specimens are introduced simultaneously into a very large system. Juveniles often shelter inside branching corals and may exhibit a characteristic stress response — lying motionless on their side — when disturbed.
breedingNotes: Not bred in the home aquarium. The species was first successfully reared in captivity in 2016 by Rising Tide Conservation in collaboration with the University of Florida Tropical Aquaculture Laboratory, after roughly six years of larval-rearing work. Commercial-scale aquaculture was achieved in early 2025, and captive-bred specimens are now available from a growing number of specialty online retailers, though home-aquarium spawning has not been reported.

fish.breedingNotes: First bred in captivity in 2016 through a collaboration between Rising Tide Conservation and the University of Florida Tropical Aquaculture Laboratory, following several years of larval-rearing work. Commercial-scale production was achieved in early 2025, and captive-bred specimens are now available through a growing number of specialty online retailers; home-aquarium spawning has not been reported.
